title: HP Inc. (HPQ) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of HP Inc. (HPQ).

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: A very conservative 2.0% growth rate is projected. This acknowledges the massive base of $2.8B in free cash flow, but tempers expectations due to the secular decline in legacy printing and the commoditized nature of the PC hardware market.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% discount rate reflects the higher risk profile associated with operating in mature, heavily commoditized hardware markets with structural headwinds in key profitable segments.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 1.0% terminal growth rate assumes the company will grow slower than inflation and GDP long-term, reflecting the mature and potentially shrinking nature of its core hardware businesses.

thesisParagraphs: HP Inc. represents a classic deep-value proposition. The market heavily discounts its massive, consistent free cash flow generation due to valid concerns regarding the structural decline of its legacy printing business and the highly commoditized, cyclical nature of the PC market. Consequently, it trades at heavily depressed multiples (trailing P/E under 7).

However, at its current valuation, the market is pricing in perpetual decline. With nearly $2.8 billion in trailing free cash flow, management's aggressive commitment to share repurchases and dividends creates a significant floor for the stock. Even with highly pessimistic growth assumptions, HP's ability to consistently generate cash makes it intrinsically undervalued, offering a substantial margin of safety for value-oriented investors.
verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why a 2% FCF growth rate for HP?
faq.answer: The 2% rate is deliberately conservative. It balances the steady cash flow of the enterprise businesses against the long-term, secular decline in high-margin printing supplies and intense PC market competition.

faq.question: What is HP's primary defense against a declining print market?
faq.answer: HP relies on massive scale and aggressive cost management. Furthermore, its aggressive capital return strategy (buybacks and dividends) ensures that the cash generated is returned to shareholders, mitigating the impact of slow top-line growth.

faq.question: Why is HP considered undervalued?
faq.answer: HP trades at a deeply discounted multiple compared to the broader market. Our model, even using pessimistic growth assumptions (1% terminal growth), yields an intrinsic value of $28.50 compared to a $18.48 current price, indicating a significant margin of safety.
